candidates.evidence_quote: Any adverse food safety occurrence may result in litigation against us.

restaurant sales as well as third-party sales of bakery products could be negatively impacted
we are subject to a comprehensive regulatory framework that governs the manufacture (including composition and ingredients), labeling, packaging and safety of food in the United States
we must comply with alcoholic beverage control regulations which require us to apply to a state or other governmental alcoholic beverage control authority for licenses and permits
Dram shop litigation may result in significant judgments, including punitive damages.
Significant increases in minimum wage rates, including the tip credit wage rate in certain states
could significantly increase our labor costs, which would materially adversely affect our financial performance
immigration-related employment regulations may make it more difficult for us to identify and hire qualified staff members
Labor organizing could harm our operations and competitive position in the restaurant industry
Adverse weather conditions, natural disasters and public health emergencies can impact customer traffic
more severe events, such as hurricanes, earthquakes, tornadoes, blizzards, wildfires and other natural disasters
public health emergencies, such as the COVID-19 pandemic, have resulted in and may in the future result in restaurant closures
Any such situation could adversely impact customer traffic and make it more difficult to fully staff our restaurants
including civil unrest, customer intimidation, active shooter situations and terrorist activities, may result in damage and restricted access to our restaurants
impediments to availability of staff and supplies and increased commodity costs, sometimes for prolonged periods of time
The aggregate impact of these and other factors contributed to significant cost inflation.
During fiscal 2021, we began to experience certain supply shortages and transportation delays largely attributable to impacts of the COVID-19 pandemic.

we implemented price increases above our historical levels to support our longer-term restaurant-level margin objectives

including the value of the U.S. dollar relative to other currencies, international trade disputes, tariffs, geopolitical unrest and varying global demand

These shortages continued in fiscal 2022 and were exacerbated by geopolitical unrest.

Because of this lack of competition, we may be vulnerable to excessive price demands, especially as they relate to the cost of products or services that are critical to our operations or profitability.
during fiscal 2022, we experienced supply shortages and significant price volatility with respect to certain sustainable products

We have a succession plan that includes short-term and long-term planning elements intended to allow us to successfully continue operations should any of our senior management become unavailable to serve in their respective roles.
we have experienced and continue to experience labor shortfalls relative to our sales levels in certain parts of our workforce
Our vendors’ systems have experienced cybersecurity breaches, including credential stuffing attacks in which compromised user credentials were used to breach the systems
The failure of third-party vendors to provide adequate services, including, as result of any Security Incident, could significantly harm our operations and reputation

either of which result could significantly impact our off-premise sales
additional costs, such as increased rent and other expenses related to renegotiating the terms of occupancy of an existing lease

Commodity price fluctuations have and may continue to impede our international licensees’ profitability
We have and may again be required to record impairment charges, be unable to fully recoup landlord improvement allowances and/or decide to discontinue operations at certain restaurants
We are also subject to various environmental regulations governing areas such as water usage, sanitation disposal and transportation mitigation.

notes: Chunk contains a single event: part 3 of 6 of the 2023-02-27 10-K (Risk Factors / regulatory section); no fiscal_kpis in this chunk (those appear only on chunk_001).
All candidates share source_id 0001104659-23-026072; quotes are verbatim substrings of the event content, including original curly quotes/apostrophes.

Skipped as vague/boilerplate or covered by other candidates: generic changes-in-laws umbrella, ADA/equal-employment compliance, joint-employer liability for third-party providers, gift-card vendor revenue dependence, M&A/brand-extension initiative risk, social-media strategy differentiation (folded into brand_reputation), infrastructure scaling / sales deleveraging, climate change as standalone (covered by weather_conditions/natural_disaster), trade embargo (covered by tariff_policy).
Person name (CEO) excluded from naming per rules; executive_succession coined from the succession-plan discussion instead.
xbrl_or_null set only where the mapping is obvious (impairment charges, operating lease cost); all others null.
